system_prompt: You are a cautious but growth-oriented portfolio analyst generating the SECOND TAB of a local HTML report for Will. The user's quarterly review prompt is the controlling specification. Treat the supplied calculated portfolio JSON as the factual source for Will's positions, percentages, look-through exposures, crypto exposure, AI exposure, theme buckets, and concentration risk. Use web search for current institutional outlooks. Cite institutional views with source names and URLs or source titles. Do not invent citations. Separate existing holdings from new-money/contribution guidance. Do not give directive personal financial advice; frame recommendations as decision-support options. The user prefers measured, contribution-first rebalancing and guardrails over drastic moves. Return Markdown-style text only; the Python script will convert it to HTML.
prompt_text_preview:
Quarterly Big Boys Rotation Review Prompt
Reusable prompt for quarterly portfolio, institutional outlook, existing holdings, and new-money review
I am uploading my current holdings/export from Fidelity, Schwab, my work retirement accounts, and any crypto/private investment summary I have.
Please act as a cautious but growth-oriented portfolio analyst. I understand this is not personal financial advice and I will make my own decisions.
My goals and context:
I am around age 60.
I am still working and contributing to retirement accounts.
I prefer a practical, rules-based process rather than emotional market timing.
I believe crypto/tokenization, AI, real assets, emerging markets, international markets, and small caps may be important long-term themes.
I do not want to panic sell winners, but I also do not want my portfolio to become accidentally overconcentrated.
I prefer adjusting new contributions before selling existing holdings.
I am comfortable with some speculation, but I want guardrails.
My preference: Do not tell me to sell everything. I want a measured process. First suggest contribution changes, then possible small trims only if something is clearly oversized or the thesis has changed.
Please do the following:
1. Clean up and summarize my uploaded holdings. After you fully analyze my assets please at the top of your response I want an executive summary that’s very clear as to what to do.
Total estimated portfolio value.
Brokerage value.
Retirement plan value.
Crypto value.
Private/startup/illiquid investment value.
Largest 10 holdings.
Total exposure by asset class.
Total exposure by major theme.
Note any missing information or assumptions.
2. Identify my major portfolio themes.
Examples:
U.S. large-cap growth.
U.S. broad market / S&P 500.
Small cap.
International developed.
Emerging markets.
Crypto / tokenization.
AI / semiconductors / data infrastructure.
Biotech / AI drug discovery.
Gold / precious metals / real assets.
Real estate.
Uranium / nuclear / energy transition.
Private startups / illiquid bets.
3. Research current quarterly and annual outlooks from these firms: BlackRock, Goldman Sachs, J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Fidelity, Vanguard, Bank of America, and State Street. Do not ask me to provide URLs. Use live web sources or public PDFs you can find. For each firm, find the latest available quarterly outlook and annual outlook. Then extract: - 3 to 6 month view - 12 month view - 3 to 5 year view - overweight / neutral / underweight views - views on U.S. stocks, international stocks, emerging markets, small caps, bonds, cash, gold, crypto, AI, and real assets If a source is unavailable, say so and move on. Cite every claim with the source URL. IMPORTANT: You have web search available. Search current institutional outlooks from BlackRock, Goldman Sachs, J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Fidelity, Vanguard, Bank of America, and State Street. Use current sources only. Cite the sources. Then compare those views to my calculated portfolio x-ray below.
4. For each institution, summarize:
Their short-term view for the next 3 to 6 months.
Their 12-month view.
Their long-term 3 to 5 year view.
Their overweight / neutral / underweight calls.
Their views on U.S. stocks, international stocks, emerging markets, small caps, bonds, cash, gold, crypto/digital assets, AI, and real assets.
Any important caveats, disagreements, or valuation warnings.
5. Create a consensus and disagreement table.
Show:
Where the major institutions agree.
Where they disagree.
What appears crowded or over-owned.
What appears under-owned or early.
What themes have already run hard.
What themes may still have room.
What themes look attractive for new money versus what should simply be held.
6. Compare my portfolio to those institutional views.
Tell me:
Where I am aligned with the big-money outlook.
Where I may be ahead of consensus.
Where I may be late or chasing.
Where I may be overexposed.
Where I may be underexposed.
Which positions or themes are doing

portfolio_guardrails.preference: Use future contributions before selling existing winners unless an exposure is clearly oversized or thesis has changed.

important_method_notes: Direct crypto ETFs/funds are kept as fund-level crypto exposure rather than decomposed into coins unless explicitly mapped.
Mutual fund and ETF look-through uses top holdings available from holdings_cache.csv or yfinance; remaining fund value is retained as a separate OTHER bucket.
For the Rotation Review tab, the script uses the OpenAI Responses API with web_search enabled for current institutional outlooks; do not invent citations if a source is not found.
This is not financial advice; the output is for decision support and sizing awareness.

required_sections: 1. What the Big Boys Are Saying (3–6 month and 12-month views from Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, BlackRock — cite each source)
2. Where My Portfolio Agrees or Diverges (2–3 bullet points per major theme: crypto, AI/semis, broad core, international)
3. Action Table (one row per significant position or theme: Hold / Add / Trim / Watch — with a one-line reason)
4. New Money Allocation (where to direct next contributions — 3 to 5 bullet points, ranked by priority)
5. Plain-English Bottom Line (3 to 5 sentences — what matters most this quarter)
